title: Volume in Seiberg-Witten theory and the existence of two Reeb orbits
speaker: Daniel Cristofaro-Gardiner
affiliation: University of California, Berkeley; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will discuss recent joint work with Vinicius Gripp and Michael Hutchings relating the volume of any contact three-manifold to the length of certain finite sets of Reeb orbits. I will also explain why this result implies that any closed contact three-manifold has at least two embedded Reeb orbits.

proof_stages.label: Contact setup and two-orbit theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a closed contact three-manifold, its Reeb vector field and contact structure, recalls the Weinstein conjecture, and states the Hutchings--speaker theorem that every contact form has at least two closed Reeb orbits without any nondegeneracy assumption.

proof_stages.label: Embedded contact homology chain complex
proof_stages.summary: For a nondegenerate contact form, ECH is reviewed as a chain complex generated by orbit sets, with differential counting ECH-index-one holomorphic curves, an index/grading package forcing embeddedness, and Hutchings--Taubes's proof that the differential squares to zero.

proof_stages.label: Taubes isomorphism and invariance
proof_stages.summary: Taubes's identification of ECH with Kronheimer--Mrowka Seiberg--Witten Floer homology is introduced to provide the key invariance: the ECH vector space depends only on the induced contact/spin-c data, not on auxiliary analytic choices.

proof_stages.label: ECH spectral invariants and degenerate extension
proof_stages.summary: The action filtration on orbit sets defines spectral invariants c_sigma(lambda) for nondegenerate forms, and scaling/monotonicity allow these invariants to extend continuously to degenerate contact forms by C^0 approximation.

proof_stages.label: Torsion grading and volume asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: After choosing a homology class making c1(xi)+2PD(gamma) torsion, ECH acquires a relative integer grading, is infinitely generated via Seiberg--Witten theory, and satisfies the Gripp--Hutchings--speaker asymptotic formula c_sigma^2/I(sigma) tending to the contact volume.

proof_stages.label: U-map and finite-orbit spectral constraints
proof_stages.summary: The ECH U-map is introduced, Seiberg--Witten periodicity supplies a U-sequence of nonzero classes, and the key lemma says that if only finitely many Reeb orbits exist then spectral values lie in the integer semigroup generated by their periods and are strictly lowered by U, even in the degenerate case.

proof_stages.label: Single-orbit contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming there is only one Reeb orbit, the finite-orbit lemma forces spectral invariants along a U-sequence to grow at least linearly in the grading, contradicting the finite volume asymptotic c_sigma^2/I(sigma), and hence at least two closed Reeb orbits must exist.

examples.name: Morse-theory nondegeneracy analogy
examples.note: The speaker compares nondegenerate Reeb orbits with nondegenerate critical points in Morse theory to clarify why the theorem's lack of a nondegeneracy hypothesis matters.
examples.engagement_reason: the Morse-theory case is only used as an analogy for nondegeneracy and degenerate critical points, with no calculation or verification in Morse theory

passes.reason: A degenerate Morse critical point has a singular Hessian and is used only as an analogy for degenerate Reeb orbits.

examples.name: Symplectic field theory multiple-cover comparison
examples.note: The comparison with SFT conventions illustrates why ECH orbit sets allow multiple covers of elliptic but not hyperbolic Reeb orbits.

examples.name: The manifold S^3 and its unique spin-c structure
examples.note: The case of S^3 is used to illustrate how Taubes's isomorphism can make ECH depend only on topological spin-c data rather than on the particular contact form.
examples.engagement_reason: S^3 is cited to illustrate that there is only one spin-c structure and hence the invariance statement becomes topological, without deriving this fact

passes.reason: For S^3 the spin-c affine space collapses to a singleton, and the example only illustrates the strength of topological invariance.
For S^3 the Spin-c torsor collapses to a singleton, and the example only illustrates the strength of topological invariance.
S^3 is maximally regular and its H^2=0 collapses the spin-c affine space to a single structure, serving only as an illustration of invariance.
Standard S^3 is maximally symmetric, and its Spin-c affine space collapses to a singleton, but it only illustrates invariance.
S^3 is the standard highly symmetric sphere and its spin-c affine space collapses to a singleton.

examples.name: Generator count with no or one Reeb orbit
examples.note: The hypothetical cases of no Reeb orbits, one hyperbolic orbit, and one elliptic orbit illustrate how infinite generation of ECH already suggests Weinstein-type consequences but does not by itself prove the two-orbit theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes what the ECH generators would be with no Reeb orbits or with a single elliptic or hyperbolic orbit and uses this to infer consequences

passes.reason: The no-or-one-Reeb-orbit generator count is a minimal/empty enemy case used to derive impossibility from infinite generation.
The no-or-one-Reeb-orbit generator count is a minimal enemy case whose finite or sparse ECH generators would contradict infinite generation.
The zero-or-one-Reeb-orbit generator count is an empty/minimal enemy case whose too-small ECH generators would contradict infinite generation.
The no-or-one-Reeb-orbit generator count is an empty/minimal lower-bound case treated as the enemy case against infinite generation.

examples.name: Period-one single-orbit spectral sequence
examples.note: The speaker normalizes a hypothetical sole orbit to period one to illustrate that strict U-map decrease forces spectral values along a U-sequence to grow like 1,2,3,4 rather than stabilizing.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the U-sequence and spectral-invariant lemma to a single period-one orbit, tracing the forced sequence of action values and the resulting linear-growth contradiction
